headline: BEANS LEAD THE COMPLEX BACK NORTH
subheadline: Soybeans ran 30 cents on USDA acreage confirmation and heat-dome weather talk; corn and wheat followed, cattle barely moved.
lead: Soybeans had their biggest single-session gain in weeks, nearby up 30¾ cents to $11.81, after USDA confirmed 85.4 million soybean acres Tuesday morning, matching March's estimate and removing the fear of a surprise acreage add. That matters for the weekly thread: Monday's question was whether Crop Progress would confirm pollination-week crop health or add a weather premium, and today's answer is both, corn conditions look solid per field reports out of Michigan and Minnesota, but an approaching Plains heat dome just enough uncertainty to keep a modest weather premium alive in December corn at $4.59. The complex is no longer in Monday's defensive crouch. The question now is whether the funds follow soybeans higher or treat today's move as a one-day cover.
the_takeaway: Soybeans reclaimed ground; whether the funds stay is Thursday's export sales story.
teaser: Beans ran hard on acreage confirmation. Corn and wheat followed. Cattle barely blinked.

one_number.context: USDA's June Acreage report confirmed 85.4 million soybean acres, unchanged from March's Prospective Plantings estimate. No surprise add means no new supply overhang to price, and that removed a ceiling the nearby contract had been rubbing against. When acreage comes in at or below the earlier estimate, the balance sheet stays as tight as the funds modeled it. Today's 30-cent move is the market pricing out the risk that it was wrong.

yesterdays_call.summary: Called beans up, toward $12.10, after Monday's Crop Progress print.
yesterdays_call.note: Nearby beans closed at $11.815 today, which means the $12.10 target did not play out -- the market moved in the right direction on the 30-cent rally but stopped well short of the level. Own the miss on the level; the directional read was correct, the target was ambitious. Thursday export sales above 400K MT is the next real look at whether the funds are still pointing that direction.

sections.title: Soybeans: Acreage Clears the Deck
sections.body: Nearby soybeans ran 30¾ cents to **$11.81**, the biggest single-session gain in several weeks, and November added 3 cents to **$11.95**. The catalyst is clean: USDA's June 2026 Acreage report confirmed 85.4 million planted soybean acres, matching March's estimate exactly. No acreage surprise means no new bushels to absorb, and the balance sheet the funds modeled at planting stays intact. The narrow old-crop/new-crop spread, nearby at $11.81 versus November at $11.95, tells you carry is still orderly and the move was not short-covering selling pressure but a real re-pricing of the supply picture. Next test is **$12.10** resistance; Thursday's weekly export sales will say whether the funds are adding conviction or just covering a short.
sections.catalyst: USDA June Acreage confirmed 85.4M soybean acres, matching March estimate, no supply surprise.
sections.bottom_line: Acreage risk is off the table; now the funds need a demand catalyst to push through $12.10.
sections.farmer_action: If you have unpriced new-crop soybeans, watch Thursday's export sales. Above 400K MT keeps the move alive toward $12.10; below 300K MT and today's run fades.
sections.vs_yesterday: Beans: reversed Monday's softness on a clean acreage catalyst.

sections.title: Corn: Heat Dome Adds a Little Back
sections.body: Nearby corn firmed 6 cents to **$4.39** and December added 1¾ cents to **$4.59**, recovering a fraction of Monday's sell-off. The 2026 planting season's corn acreage came in at 95.3 million acres per Tuesday's USDA report, also matching March's estimate, which is modestly supportive on its own. What added a bit more was weather: an ag meteorologist noted the Plains heat dome in the forecast is not expected to cause widespread pollination damage as long as the ridge stays centered there rather than drifting east into the heart of the Corn Belt. That assessment kept a weather premium from building meaningfully, but it did not fully evaporate it either. December at **$4.59** is holding well above the $4.35 line that has come up repeatedly this week; a sustained close below that level is the signal to revisit storage economics.
sections.catalyst: USDA Acreage confirmed 95.3M corn acres; Plains heat dome assessed as limited pollination risk by ag meteorologist.
sections.bottom_line: Corn steadied on acreage confirmation and a muted heat-dome read; $4.59 December is the level to watch.
sections.vs_yesterday: Corn: bounced off Monday's lows but hasn't reclaimed $4.42 nearby yet.

sections.title: Wheat Follows, Does Not Lead
sections.body: Chicago wheat added 12½ cents to **$6.06**, a 2.1% gain that puts it back inside last week's range. Wheat's move today is real but it is riding the broader grain complex lift rather than generating its own catalyst. Field reports out of Minnesota show wheat fully headed with timely rains, which means the supply story is not getting worse. The overnight magnitude was 1.1x normal, mild by overnight standards but still notable coming off a quiet extended weekend. Next resistance is **$6.20**; below that, wheat is still the follower in this complex, not the leader.
sections.catalyst: Complex-wide grain lift on USDA acreage confirmation; Minnesota field reports show wheat headed with timely rains.
sections.bottom_line: Wheat reclaimed ground on complex strength, not its own catalyst; $6.20 is the first real test.

sections.title: Cattle: Barely a Pulse
sections.body: Live cattle settled at **$239.38**, up just 0.1%, and feeders held at **$360.82**, unchanged. The ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler plant lockout, which began May 19 and removed roughly 2% of weekly US slaughter capacity, continues to create processing-constrained price dynamics. Box-beef demand from ground beef remained firm per Beef Magazine reporting, with ground beef now making up half of total consumption as imports and heavier weights partially offset lower slaughter. The cattle complex is not reacting to today's grain move, which is notable: corn up 6 cents on the nearby should pressure feeders, and the fact that feeders held flat says the market is either already pricing corn's level or is waiting on the next Cattle on Feed before repositioning. No resolution on the Cargill lockout means no clean upside catalyst for live cattle.
sections.catalyst: Cargill Fort Morgan lockout (ongoing since May 19) keeps processing-constrained dynamics in place; ground beef demand steady.
sections.bottom_line: Cattle holding but not moving; the Cargill lockout is the ceiling, not a floor.

sections.title: Energy: Hormuz Premium Keeps Unwinding
sections.body: WTI crude settled at **$69.23**, up just 0.4%, as the Iran-Hormuz tensions, with the Strait of Hormuz premium that built since early April now deflating on diplomatic progress, continued their slow grind lower in risk. Indian state refiners returned to buying Iraqi crude transiting the Strait, and two large Saudi crude carriers are heading through, both signs that the chokepoint is open for business. Gulf producers cut official selling prices sharply to attract Asian buyers, which tells you supply is competing on price, not rationing by availability. For ag producers, the relevant line is diesel input costs: crude at $69 is meaningfully lower than the $83 range of early May, and that gap is slowly working through to farm-level input pricing. Natural gas at **$3.27** added 1.3%, still at the low end of its 52-week range.
sections.catalyst: India resumes Iraqi crude imports via Hormuz; Saudi crude carriers clearing the Strait; Gulf producers cutting official selling prices.
sections.bottom_line: Crude holding near $69 as Hormuz risk unwinds; diesel input relief is real and building.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA Launches $500M Domestic Fertilizer Expansion Program
outside_the_pit.body: Secretary Rollins announced a $500 million Fertilizer Investment Expansion for Long-Term Domestic Supply Program through USDA Rural Development, aimed at reducing import dependence after Iran-related price pressure earlier this year. This does not move today's nitrogen market, but it is the kind of structural supply-side investment that shows up in input cost projections two to three crop cycles out.

outside_the_pit.title: DFA Idling St. Albans Milk Plant, 81 Jobs Cut August 17
outside_the_pit.body: Dairy Farmers of America's St. Albans, Vermont plant closes August 17, with a union reviewing the decision. Class III Milk at $15.60 is already at the low end of its 52-week range; regional plant closures that reduce processing capacity in the Northeast are the kind of structural tightening that does not show up in futures immediately but pressures basis for Northeast dairy producers.

outside_the_pit.title: Illinois Crops Hit by Tornadoes, Hail, and Severe Weather This Season
outside_the_pit.body: The Illinois FSA director told Brownfield that the state has seen damaging tornadoes, hail, and multiple severe weather events this growing season, with crop conditions impacted across multiple districts. While today's complex-wide grain rally was driven by acreage confirmation rather than weather fear, Illinois damage adds to the patchwork of localized yield drag that aggregate condition scores can understate.

spread_to_watch.label: Soybeans nearby / November '26 spread
spread_to_watch.level: $0.14 inverse, nearby above new-crop, narrowed from recent weeks
spread_to_watch.commentary: Nearby beans at $11.81 versus November at $11.95 is a 14-cent carry, meaning new-crop is slightly above old-crop, which is the normal structure for this time of year. What to watch is whether today's 30-cent run in nearby pulled new-crop along or left it behind: November added only 3 cents versus old-crop's 30¾, which says the acreage-confirmation rally is being credited more to old-crop tightness than a new-crop supply story. If November starts closing the gap toward old-crop, that is the funds expressing conviction in next year's balance sheet.

basis.headline: Soybean basis firming in Eastern Belt on acreage news.
basis.body: Eastern Belt soybean basis is **tightening** as merchandisers respond to the acreage confirmation and try to move remaining old-crop bushels before the new-crop pipeline opens. Corn basis in the Eastern Belt remains firm as ethanol demand stays steady. Western Belt corn basis is softer, consistent with the seasonal pattern and adequate supplies moving through country elevators. Wheat basis is quiet on both ends of the Belt with harvest pressure working its way through.

weekly_thread.question: Does today's Crop Progress print confirm pollination-week crop health, or add a weather premium that pulls corn back above $4.35?
weekly_thread.status_text: Tuesday's USDA Acreage report confirmed 95.3M corn acres and 85.4M soybean acres, matching March estimates, which removed the acreage-risk ceiling and let soybeans run. The Plains heat dome is in the forecast but an ag meteorologist assessed its pollination impact as limited, so the weather premium is alive but not building aggressively. December corn at $4.59 has reclaimed ground above $4.35; the question now shifts to whether Thursday's export sales sustain the move or expose it as a one-day cover.

the_more_you_know.title: 95.3 Million Acres: Why the 'No Surprise' Acreage Report Is the Bullish Event
the_more_you_know.body: Today's USDA Acreage confirmation at 95.3 million corn acres and 85.4 million soybean acres matched March exactly, and soybeans ran 30¾ cents on it. That seems backwards until you understand how the market prices acreage risk in the weeks before the report. Funds and merchandisers build a probability distribution around the March estimate: maybe acres come in 500,000 higher, maybe lower. That uncertainty is a real cost, and it gets priced into the carry structure as a discount. When the report comes in at the midpoint of that distribution, the uncertainty premium breaks down, and the market re-prices to a world where the balance sheet is exactly as tight as everyone thought. Today's soybean move is not the market saying conditions got better; it is the market saying a bearish scenario it was partially pricing did not happen.

watch_list.desc: USDA Weekly Export Sales: soybeans above 400K MT keeps today's rally alive toward $12.10; below 300K MT and the run fades back toward $11.60.
Plains heat dome track: if the ridge drifts east of the Kansas-Nebraska line into Illinois and Indiana during pollination week, weather premium in December corn above $4.70 becomes the story.
USDA Crop Progress: corn good/excellent rating above 70% removes weather premium; below 65% on a heat-dome week adds it back fast.
Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler lockout resolution: any announcement of a settlement reopens 6,000 head per day of slaughter capacity and is an immediate catalyst for live cattle above $242.

daily_quote.text: No winter lasts forever; no spring skips its turn.

source_summary: USDA June 2026 Acreage Report; Brownfield Ag News; Farm Policy News (Illinois); Beef Magazine; Feedstuffs; OilPrice.com; The Fence Post; CME Group settlement prices.
